docs.title: Nodes Meta Commands
docs.text: The nodes meta commands deal with SolarNode metadata, which is metadata associated with a specific node. Node metadata is both published and consumed by nodes. For example nodes publish metadata about themselves (like hardware architecture and version); node metadata can be easily used in node expressions, which makes it a good candidate for node-level parameters that do not change too frequently.

docs.title: Nodes Meta CSV-Get
docs.text: Extract CSV from node metadata at a specific metadata key path. CSV metadata is often used for tariff schedules in SolarNode, such as tariff schedule expressions.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
s10k nodes meta csv-get [-mode=<displayMode>] -node=<nodeId>
                        [-path=<metadataPath>]

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Long Version Description -node= --node-id= the node ID to get metadata from -path= --path= the key path with the CSV data to extract -mode= --display-mode= the format to display the data as, one of CSV, JSON, or PRETTY; defaults to PRETTY

docs.title: Output
docs.text: The CSV data, formatted according to the --display-mode option.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Show CSV data in the default formatting:

Show CSV with pretty formattingOutput 
s10k nodes meta csv-get --node-id 101 --path /pm/cpd/schedule

+-----------------+-----+---------+------+-----------------+
|           Month | Day | Weekday | Time | cpdPeriodActive |
+-----------------+-----+---------+------+-----------------+
| Jan-Apr,Oct-Dec |     |         |      |               0 |
+-----------------+-----+---------+------+-----------------+
|         May-Sep |     |         |      |               1 |
+-----------------+-----+---------+------+-----------------+

The same data can be output as CSV as well:

Show CSV with CSV formattingOutput 
s10k nodes meta csv-get --node-id 101 --path /pm/cpd/schedule \
     --display-mode CSV

Month,Day,Weekday,Time,cpdPeriodActive
"Jan-Apr,Oct-Dec",,,,0
May-Sep,,,,1

The same data can be output as JSON as well:

Show CSV with JSON formattingOutput 
s10k nodes meta csv-get --node-id 101 --path /pm/cpd/schedule \
     --display-mode JSON

[
  [ "Month", "Day", "Weekday", "Time", "cpdPeriodActive" ],
  [ "Jan-Apr,Oct-Dec", null, null, null, "0" ],
  [ "May-Sep", null, null, null, "1" ]
]

docs.title: Nodes Meta CSV-Set
docs.text: Save CSV data to node metadata at a specific metadata key path. CSV metadata is often used for tariff schedules in SolarNode, such as tariff schedule expressions.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
s10k nodes meta csv-set [-s] [-mode=<displayMode>] -node=<nodeId>
                        [-path=<metadataPath>] [<csv>]

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Long Version Description -node= --node-id= the node ID to get metadata from -path= --path= the key path with the CSV data to extract -mode= --display-mode= if --verbose then the format to display the saved data as, one of CSV, JSON, or PRETTY; defaults to PRETTY -s --string encode the CSV as a JSON string, intead of JSON arrays

docs.title: Output
docs.text: A success message. If the --verbose option specified then this will be followed by a pretty-printed copy of the saved CSV data, formatted according to the --display-mode option.

docs.title: String mode
docs.text:
The --string option lets you encode the CSV data as a metadata string value, rather than the default method of encoding it as arrays. For example take this sample CSV data:

Month,Day,Weekday,Time,cpdPeriodActive
"Jan-Apr,Oct-Dec",,,,0
May-Sep,,,,1

Here is how it would be saved as metadata at the key path /pm/cpd/schedule normally, and with the --string option:

Normal CSV metadataString CSV metadata 
{
    "pm" : {
        "cpd" : {
            "schedule" : [
                [ "Month", "Day", "Weekday", "Time", "cpdPeriodActive" ],
                [ "Jan-Apr,Oct-Dec", null, null, null, "0" ],
                [ "May-Sep", null, null, null, "1" ]
            ]
        }
    }
}

{
    "pm" : {
        "cpd" : {
            "schedule" : "Month,Day,Weekday,Time,cpdPeriodActive\r\n\"Jan-Apr,Oct-Dec\",,,,0\r\nMay-Sep,,,,1"
        }
    }
}

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
The data to save can be provided directly as a command argument, for example:

CSV as command argument
s10k nodes meta csv-set --node-id 101 \
  --path /pm/cpd/schedule 'Key,Value\nA,B'

A file with the data can be referenced using @@ followed by the file path:

CSV as a file
s10k nodes meta csv-set --node-id 101 \
  --path /pm/cpd/schedule @@/path/to/data.csv

The data can be read from standard input, like this:

CSV read from standard input
s10k nodes meta csv-set --node-id 101 \
  --path /pm/cpd/schedule </path/to/data.csv

Similarly, the metadata content can be piped to the command, like this:

ExampleOutput Metadata piped from standard input
cat /path/to/data.csv |s10k --verbose --profile demo nodes meta csv-set \
  --node-id 101 --path /pm/cpd/schedule --display-mode JSON

CSV saved to to path [/pm/cpd/schedule].
[
  [ "Month", "Day", "Weekday", "Time", "cpdPeriodActive" ],
  [ "Jan-Apr,Oct-Dec", null, null, null, "0" ],
  [ "May-Sep", null, null, null, "1" ]
]

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: s10k nodes meta delete -node=<nodeId>

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Long Version Description -node= --node-id= the node ID to delete metadata from

docs.title: Output
docs.text: A success message.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Delete metadata
s10k nodes meta delete --node-id 101

docs.title: Nodes Meta List
docs.text: Show metadata matching a search filter.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
s10k nodes meta list [-filter=<filter>] -node=nodeId[,nodeId...]
                     [-node=nodeId[,nodeId...]]...
                     [-mode=<displayMode>]

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Long Version Description -node= --node-id= the node ID(s) to show metadata for -filter= --filter= an optional metadata filter to limit results to -mode= --display-mode= the format to display the data as, one of CSV, JSON, or PRETTY; defaults to PRETTY

docs.title: Output
docs.text: A listing of all matching node metadata records.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
You can show exactly the metadata for a single node like this:

Show metadata for one nodeOutput 
s10k nodes meta list --node-id 101

+---------+-----------------------------+-------------------+
| Node ID | Updated                     | Metadata          |
+---------+-----------------------------+-------------------+
|     101 | 2025-08-30T03:36:06.277076Z | {                 |
|         |                             |   "m" : {         |
|         |                             |     "limit" : 123 |
|         |                             |   }               |
|         |                             | }                 |
+---------+-----------------------------+-------------------+

You can show multiple node metadatas by providing multiple --node-id options or as a comma-delimited list:

Show metadata for multiple nodesOutput 
# using multiple options
s10k nodes meta list --node-id 101 --node-id 102

# or the same thing, using a comma-delimited list
s10k nodes meta list --node-id 101,102

+---------+-----------------------------+-------------------+
| Node ID | Updated                     | Metadata          |
+---------+-----------------------------+-------------------+
|     101 | 2025-08-30T03:36:06.277076Z | {                 |
|         |                             |   "m" : {         |
|         |                             |     "limit" : 123 |
|         |                             |   }               |
|         |                             | }                 |
+---------+-----------------------------+-------------------+
|     102 | 2025-08-30T03:36:06.277076Z | {                 |
|         |                             |   "m" : {         |
|         |                             |     "limit" : 234 |
|         |                             |   }               |
|         |                             | }                 |
+---------+-----------------------------+-------------------+

You can restrict the returned metadata using a metadata filter, for example to show only the metadata where limit is greater than 200:

Show metadata for one nodeOutput 
s10k nodes meta list --node-id 101,102 --filter '(m/limit>200)'

+---------+-----------------------------+-------------------+
| Node ID | Updated                     | Metadata          |
+---------+-----------------------------+-------------------+
|     102 | 2025-08-30T03:36:06.277076Z | {                 |
|         |                             |   "m" : {         |
|         |                             |     "limit" : 234 |
|         |                             |   }               |
|         |                             | }                 |
+---------+-----------------------------+-------------------+

docs.title: Nodes Meta Save
docs.text: Save metadata associated with a node.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: s10k nodes meta save [-r] -node=<nodeId> [<metadata>]

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Long Version Description -node= --node-id= the node ID to save metadata to -r --replace replace all existing metadata, rather than add/update

docs.title: Output
docs.text: A success message, followed by a pretty-printed copy of the saved metadata.

docs.title: Metadata structure
docs.text:
SolarNetwork defines metadata as two tree-like structures along with a set of tags. First there is the m structure that defines a simple key-value pair of simple values (like strings, booleans, and numbers). Then there is the pm structure that defines an arbitrarily-nested tree structure. Then there is the t set of tags. An example looks like this:

{
  "m" : {
    "limit" : 10000,
    "group" : "alpha"
  },
  "pm" : {
    "os" : {
      "arch" : "aarch64",
      "name" : "Linux",
      "version" : "6.1.21-v8+"
    }
  },
  "t" : ["west", "red"]
}

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
The metadata to save can be provided directly as a command argument, for example:

Metadata as command argument
s10k nodes meta save --node-id 101 '{"m":{"limit":123}}'

A file with the metadata can be referenced using @@ followed by the file path:

Metadata as a file
s10k nodes meta save --node-id 101 @@/path/to/metadata.json

The metadata content can be read from standard input, like this:

Metadata read from standard input
s10k nodes meta save --node-id 101 </path/to/metadata.json

Similarly, the metadata content can be piped to the command, like this:

ExampleOutput Metadata piped from standard input
jq -n --argjson  limit 123 '{"m":{"limit":$limit}}' \
  |s10k nodes meta save --node-id 101

Node metadata added:
{
    "m" : {
        "limit" : 123
    }
}

docs.title: Sec-Tokens Commands
docs.text:
The sec-tokens group of commands deal with SolarNetwork security token related actions.

Note

All sec-tokens commands require a User security token to operate.

docs.title: Sec-Tokens Create
docs.text: Create a new security token.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
s10k sec-tokens create [-r] -t=<tokenType> [-m=<name>]
                        [-D=<description>] [-node=nodeId[,nodeId...]]...
                        [-source=sourceId[,sourceId...]]...
                        [-N=metaPath[,metaPath...]]...
                        [-U=metaPath[,metaPath...]]...
                        [-A=path[,path...]]...
                        [-exp=<expirationDate>] [-tz=<zone>]
                        [-mode=<displayMode>]
                        [-agg=aggregation |
                            --aggregation=aggregation[,aggregation...]...]
                        [-loc=precision |
                            --location-precision=precision[,precision...]...]

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Long Version Description -A= --api-path= an API path(s) to restrict access to (wildcard patterns allowed) -agg= --min-aggregation= a minimum aggregation to restrict access to --aggregation= an aggregation to restrict access to -D= --description= a description for the token -exp= --expiration-date= an expiration date for the token,like 2020-10-30 or 2020-10-30T12:45 -loc= --min-location-precision= a minimum location precision to restrict access to --location-precision= a location precision to restrict access to -m= --name= a brief name for the token -N= --node-metadata-path= node metadata paths to restrict access to (wildcard patterns allowed) -node= --node-id= node ID(s) to restrict access to -r --refresh-allowed allow signing keys for the token to be refreshed -source= --source-id= source ID(s) to restrict access to -t= --type= the type of token to create, one of ReadNodeData or User -tz= --time-zone= a time zone ID to treat expiration date as instead of the local time zone, like Pacific/Auckland or -05:00 or UTC -U= --user-metadata-path= user metadata paths to restrict access to (wildcard patterns allowed) -mode= --display-mode= the format to display the data as, one of CSV, JSON, or PRETTY; defaults to PRETTY

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
The new security token, with its associated secret.

Warning

You must copy the token secret to a safe place, as SolarNetwork will not show you its value ever again.

docs.title: Sec-Tokens Update
docs.text: Update a security token.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
s10k sec-tokens update [-rR] -id=tokenId [-m=<name>]
                        [-D=<description>] [-node=nodeId[,nodeId...]]...
                        [-source=sourceId[,sourceId...]]...
                        [-N=metaPath[,metaPath...]]...
                        [-U=metaPath[,metaPath...]]...
                        [-A=path[,path...]]...
                        [-exp=<expirationDate>] [-tz=<zone>]
                        [-mode=<displayMode>]
                        [-a | -d]
                        [-agg=aggregation |
                            --aggregation=aggregation[,aggregation...]...]
                        [-loc=precision |
                            --location-precision=precision[,precision...]...]

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Long Version Description -a --active make the token active -A= --api-path= an API path(s) to restrict access to (wildcard patterns allowed) -agg= --min-aggregation= a minimum aggregation to restrict access to --aggregation= an aggregation to restrict access to -d --disabled make the token disabled -D= --description= a description for the token -exp= --expiration-date= an expiration date for the token,like 2020-10-30 or 2020-10-30T12:45 -id= --identifier= the ID of the token to update -loc= --min-location-precision= a minimum location precision to restrict access to --location-precision= a location precision to restrict access to -m= --name= a brief name for the token -N= --node-metadata-path= node metadata paths to restrict access to (wildcard patterns allowed) -node= --node-id= node ID(s) to restrict access to -r --refresh-allowed allow signing keys for the token to be refreshed -R --replace replace the security policy, instead of updating (adding to) the policy -source= --source-id= source ID(s) to restrict access to -t= --type= the type of token to create, one of ReadNodeData or User -tz= --time-zone= a time zone ID to treat expiration date as instead of the local time zone, like Pacific/Auckland or -05:00 or UTC -U= --user-metadata-path= user metadata paths to restrict access to (wildcard patterns allowed) -mode= --display-mode= the format to display the data as, one of CSV, JSON, or PRETTY; defaults to PRETTY
